# tokens: # def-node-binding — what `def x <node>` binds, and how inner words
# resolve. After the def-list flip, a List binds exactly like a Map:
# the node is EVALUATED at def time and bound as a VALUE. The explicit
# `def name word <node>` form (an __SP splice marker) restores the old
# Forth-style splice / late-binding behaviour.
# Runs against the production lang registry (test/go/langspec).
# Expected values use the canon renderer (containers space-separated).
# 1. `def x <node>` binds a VALUE — scalar, list and map alike
# A List is no longer spliced: it binds like a Map. `word` splices.
def x 42 x
note: scalar binds a value

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] x
note: a LIST binds the list value (was: spliced)

# tokens: def x {a:1 b:2} x
note: a MAP binds the map value (unchanged)

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] size x
note: the value is a real List — size works

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] typeof x
note: …and typeof sees a List

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] x get 0
note: …and indexing works

# tokens: def x [1,2,3] x each [add 10]
note: …and higher-order words work
the list is one element when re-collected

# tokens: # Splice on demand with `word`:
def x word [1,2,3] x
note: `word` splices the elements (the old default)

# tokens: # 2. Inner words are evaluated EAGERLY at def time (list == map)
def x [1 add 2] x
note: the list body is evaluated at def time

# tokens: def c1 10 def x [c1 2 3] x
note: inner word resolves to its value at def time

# tokens: def c1 10 def x {a:c1 b:2} x
note: a map resolves the same way

# tokens: def x word [foo 2 3] x
note: a `word` splice executes the unknown inner word → errors

# tokens: # 3. Scope: value-bind = EARLY snapshot; `word` splice = LATE/dynamic
# A bound list (like a map) snapshots its words at def time.
def c1 10 def x [c1 2] def c1 20 x
note: redefining c1 does NOT change the snapshot

# tokens: def c1 10 def x [c1] undef c1 x
note: the snapshot survives undef of the source word

# tokens: def c1 10 def m {a:c1} def c1 20 m
note: maps behave identically (early snapshot)

# tokens: # A `word` splice re-resolves at every reference (late / dynamic).
def c1 10 def x word [c1 2] def c1 20 x
note: splice sees the NEW binding

# tokens: def c1 10 def x word [c1] undef c1 x
note: splice word resolved at use; once undef'd it errors

# tokens: # No closures: a returned list ignores the fn param, falling back to the
# module binding (a `=>` lambda, by contrast, captures it).
def c1 1 def mk fn [[c1:Integer] [List] [[c1]]] mk 9
note: returned list resolves c1 to the module binding, not the param

# tokens: def mk fn [[c1:Integer] [Function] [([] => [c1])]] def f (mk 7) f
note: contrast: a `=>` lambda captures the param

# tokens: # 4. Nesting: value-bind nests as data; `word` splice flattens
def a [1,2] def b [a 3] b
note: `a` is the list value, so `b` nests it

# tokens: def a word [1,2] def b word [a 3] b
note: `word` splices recursively and flattens
